      The bad chats
   =20
 =20
Do you think JPMorgan=C2=A0was doing a lot of crimes in the group chat? I d=
on=E2=80=99t especially, but I guess the point is we have no way of knowing=
. Last week U.S.=C2=A0regulators fined JPMorgan Chase & Co. a total of $200=
 million for violating record-keeping requirements. From the Securities and=
 Exchange Commission=E2=80=99s announcement:As described in the SEC=E2=80=
=99s order, JPMS admitted that from at least January 2018 through November =
2020, its employees often communicated about securities business matters on=
 their personal devices, using text messages, WhatsApp, and personal email =
accounts. None of these records were preserved by the firm as required by t=
he federal securities laws. JPMS further admitted that these failures were =
firm-wide and that practices were not hidden within the firm. Indeed, super=
visors, including managing directors and other senior supervisors =E2=80=93=
 the very people responsible for implementing and ensuring compliance with =
JPMS=E2=80=99s policies and procedures =E2=80=93 used their personal device=
s to communicate about the firm=E2=80=99s securities business.JPMS received=
 both subpoenas for documents and voluntary requests from SEC staff in nume=
rous investigations during the time period that the firm failed to maintain=
 required records. In responding to these subpoenas and requests, JPMS freq=
uently did not search for relevant records contained on the personal device=
s of its employees. JPMS acknowledged that its recordkeeping failures depri=
ved the SEC staff of timely access to evidence and potential sources of inf=
ormation for extended periods of time and in some instances permanently. As=
 such, the firm=E2=80=99s actions meaningfully impacted the SEC=E2=80=99s a=
bility to investigate potential violations of the federal securities laws.T=
he SEC took $125 million from JPMorgan for this; the Commodity Futures Trad=
ing Commission took another $75 million for similar violations. Were they d=
iscussing crimes in these unsupervised chats? Well, there were=C2=A0=E2=80=
=9Cnumerous investigations=E2=80=9D during this period, so I guess the SEC =
(and CFTC) thought they at least might have been doing some bad stuff. Cert=
ainly there is a=C2=A0history=C2=A0of banks doing bad stuff in chats; again=
 from the SEC announcement:=E2=80=9CSince the 1930s, recordkeeping and book=
s-and-records obligations have been an essential part of market integrity a=
nd a foundational component of the SEC=E2=80=99s ability to be an effective=
 cop on the beat. As technology changes, it=E2=80=99s even more important t=
hat registrants ensure that their communications are appropriately recorded=
 and are not conducted outside of official channels in order to avoid marke=
t oversight,=E2=80=9D said SEC Chair Gary Gensler. =E2=80=9CUnfortunately, =
in the past we=E2=80=99ve seen violations in the financial markets that wer=
e committed using unofficial communications channels, such as the foreign e=
xchange scandal of 2013.=E2=80=9D[1]And of course the SEC didn=E2=80=99t ge=
t to see all those texts and personal emails and WhatsApp messages, so it d=
oesn=E2=80=99t know if they were about crimes and neither do I. But the SEC=
 did manage to get at least thousands and thousands of those messages, and =
from its summary they sound fine?For example, an executive director and co-=
supervisor of the high grade credit trading desk launched a WhatsApp group =
chat entitled =E2=80=9CPortfolio Trading/auto ex=E2=80=9D on April 24, 2019=
, and invited the other nineteen members of the trading desk to join.From A=
pril 24 through December 16, 2019, at least 1,100 messages were sent among =
the chat group. Nearly all of the chat messages concerned the firm=E2=80=99=
s securities business, including investment strategy; discussions of client=
 meetings; and communications about market color, analysis, activity trends=
 or events.In addition, from at least November 2019 through November 2020, =
an executive director who worked on the capital markets desk at JPMorgan te=
xted with more than a hundred JPMorgan colleagues in various areas of the b=
roker-dealer including the investment bank, and with dozens of managing dir=
ectors and heads of several business lines.The executive director also text=
ed with dozens of JPMorgan=E2=80=99s customers, clients, third-party advise=
rs, and market participants.In all, this executive director texted more tha=
n 2,400 times in the one-year period. His texts discussed various aspects o=
f the high yield and leveraged loan capital markets business at JPMorgan, i=
ncluding the terms of the transactions JPMorgan was negotiating for its cus=
tomers, and the timing and status of these transactions.Further, in connect=
ion with work performed on behalf of an investment banking client between a=
t least January 2018 and November 2019, JPMorgan employees, including desk =
heads, managing directors, and other senior executives sent and received mo=
re than 21,000 securities business-related text and email messages using un=
approved communications methods on their personal devices. These messages w=
ere not preserved by JPMorgan.The messages reflect extensive discussion bet=
ween and among senior-level JPMorgan executives and employees, customers, c=
lients, third-party advisers, and other market participants about debt and =
equity underwriting issues.=C2=A0Do you think they also sometimes sent each=
 other crude jokes or gossiped about colleagues or made fun of clients? Bec=
ause if they did, the SEC doesn=E2=80=99t mention it. It sounds like these =
chats were earnest and businesslike and honestly kind of boring.Imagine bei=
ng the=C2=A0executive director=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0despite the name, not a =
particularly high-ranking job! =E2=80=94=C2=A0who was=C2=A0called out in a =
$200 million fine=C2=A0for texting his clients and colleagues about work. T=
his guy is not a criminal mastermind. This is a guy whose job is to type el=
ectronic messages about bond deals, and he=C2=A0did his job, but he typed t=
he messages in the wrong box.There is a model of compliance in which you do=
 a lot of formalized compliance stuff to reduce the risk of doing crimes. I=
f you fail to do the formalized compliance stuff, you might do crimes, and =
if you do=C2=A0crimes you will get in bad trouble. But the giant banks are =
too big and too regulated for that model. In giant banks, the model is that=
 the formalized compliance stuff becomes an end in itself, and if you don=
=E2=80=99t do it right=C2=A0you can=C2=A0be fined=C2=A0two hundred million =
dollars=C2=A0even if you=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0also do substantive crimes=
. This is partly because, if your bank is big enough,=C2=A0somebody is alwa=
ys doing crimes, so formalized compliance programs are the way to distingui=
sh =E2=80=9Ca few bad actors did crimes but we tried to stop them=E2=80=9D =
from =E2=80=9Cwe have a culture of doing crimes.=E2=80=9D But it is also be=
cause the formalized compliance stuff is very legible to regulators and ver=
y easy to catch.I just want to stress how far this is from a =E2=80=9Cmove =
fast and break things=E2=80=9D model. There are tons of startups and tech c=
ompanies and crypto projects that have under-invested in compliance and for=
mality and record-keeping, and have justified it by saying =E2=80=9Cwe have=
 a good culture and trust our people to do the right thing without a lot of=
 rules,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cit=E2=80=99s better to ask forgiveness than to=
 ask permission,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cehhhh those laws are pretty antiquate=
d, what are the odds that they apply to us?=E2=80=9D And here are JPMorgan=
=E2=80=99s bankers very earnestly discussing deals with colleagues and clie=

      How much coal?
   =20
 =20
You could have a model of environmental, social and governance investing in=
 which:=09There are ESG investors who own virtuous assets and do virtuous t=
hings with them, and=09There are non-ESG investors who own evil assets and =
do evil things with them.As an ESG investors, you can feel virtuous about t=
his, but you might also worry about its effect on the world. Sure you=C2=A0=
aren=E2=80=99t funding evil, but evil is getting funded. If the evil assets=
 are in evil hands, they will be used for more evil. Isn=E2=80=99t it bette=
r for ESG investors to own the evil assets and do=C2=A0virtuous=C2=A0things=
 with them?Specifically: If all the ESG-focused investors divest from therm=
al coal, and they get all of the ESG-curious energy companies they own to d=
ivest from thermal coal, then the thermal coal mines will end up in the han=
ds of investors who don=E2=80=99t care about ESG. And maybe those miners wi=
ll have a higher cost of capital and won=E2=80=99t be able to do business w=
ith some customers, but as long as there is still demand for thermal coal t=
hey will mine it. And because they don=E2=80=99t care about ESG they will m=
ine lots of it really fast, and probably=C2=A0in unsafe ways, and dump thei=
r waste in the river, and so forth.Maybe it would be better for the ESG-foc=
used investors to buy the coal mines and shut them down? The problem here i=
s that it is hard to go out to ESG investors to be like =E2=80=9Cyou should=
 spend $1 billion on this coal mine with an expected return of zero.=E2=80=
=9D ESG investing aims to produce good sustainable financial returns; it is=
 not pure philanthropy. If=C2=A0ESG investors buy=C2=A0a coal mine, they wi=
ll expect to make a profit. It=E2=80=99s not easy to make a profit by buyin=
g coal mines and shutting them down.=C2=A0It seems to me that this is a ver=
y hard problem, but good for=C2=A0Citigroup Inc. for giving it a try:The ba=
nk teamed up with commodities trader Trafigura Group Pte. Ltd. and Resource=
 Capital Funds, a private-equity firm, to pitch an investment vehicle earli=
er this year known as Coal to Zero. The fund planned to buy mines in the U.=
S., Australia and South Africa and run them with the promise of shutting th=
em down by 2040, according to people familiar with the matter and a marketi=
ng document seen by The Wall Street Journal.The fund was trying to solve a =
thorny problem in the green-energy world. Some energy and mining companies =
have divested coal assets under the pretense of cutting carbon emissions or=
 appeasing shareholders, only to sell them on to owners happy to run them i=
ndefinitely. The fixed end date was a compromise. The mines could operate f=
or a time, but would limit how much coal could be extracted.Profits in the =
fund would have been split between the fund=E2=80=99s investors and initiat=
ives to create other types of employment following the shutdown of the mine=
s in coal-rich regions such as West Virginia. The fund stood out from other=
 efforts to finance the transition from coal because it targeted mines rath=
er than power plants and had no government financial backing.The fund would=
 have kept 75% of coal reserves at the mines it bought in the ground, accor=
ding to the marketing document and one of the people. It was meant to buy t=
hermal coal, the highest-emitting source of electricity generation.But it i=
s in fact a=C2=A0hard problem, the investors said no, and Citi abandoned th=
e plan. When you go to an ESG fund manager and say =E2=80=9Chey wanna buy u=
p all the coal mines=E2=80=9D she is going to say =E2=80=9Cwhat no absolute=
ly not.=E2=80=9D And then you say =E2=80=9Cno hear me out, you are a better=
 owner of coal mines than the alternative, this is better for the climate i=
n the long run,=E2=80=9D and you explain how, and she says =E2=80=9Chmm yes=
 your logic is impeccable,=E2=80=9D and you say =E2=80=9Cso you=E2=80=99re =
in?=E2=80=9D and she says =E2=80=9Cno absolutely not=E2=80=9D:Some pension =
funds and other potential investors balked at the investment vehicle becaus=
e they didn=E2=80=99t want to face criticism for investing in fossil-fuel p=
rojects despite the fund=E2=80=99s goal of accelerating the retirement of c=
oal mines, according to some of the people familiar with the matter. The gr=
oup=E2=80=99s failure to bring financial backers on board demonstrates how =
taboo thermal coal has become in the investment world.The people who would =
be good stewards of coal mines are the people who never ever want to own a =
coal mine. Oh well!

Enterprise-software-plus-a-pot-of-Bitcoins company MicroStrategy Inc. had a=
n investor day last week and said two sort of interesting things. One is ab=
out Bitcoin lending; Bloomberg=E2=80=99s  Paula Seligson reports:MicroStrat=
egy Inc. -- the enterprise software company that made waves by issuing debt=
 to buy Bitcoin -- is looking for ways to generate yield on its massive dig=
ital-coin holdings.=C2=A0Chief Executive Officer Michael Saylor discussed t=
he strategy Thursday on the company=E2=80=99s investor day call with shareh=
olders. The Bitcoin evangelist has been using cash flows from MicroStrategy=
=E2=80=99s main business to buy the cryptocurrency. It also issued two conv=
ertible bonds and a secured junk bond in addition to filing an equity shelf=
 registration to raise money to acquire the digital coin. MicroStrategy now=
 owns more than 122,000 tokens, worth in excess of $5.6 billion at current =
prices.While Saylor said the company hasn=E2=80=99t taken any formal steps =
yet toward putting its Bitcoin cache to use, he added that =E2=80=9Cthere m=
ay be opportunities to either put a mortgage against it and generate long-t=
erm debt under favorable circumstances, which we could leverage up against =
the Bitcoin, or we think that we could lend it to a trustworthy counterpart=
y.=E2=80=9DMicroStrategy has levered up to buy Bitcoins, but it hasn=E2=80=
=99t=C2=A0levered up its Bitcoins, if you know what I mean. Their plans see=
m sort of miscellaneous; the gist is that MicroStrategy has more ability to=
 manage its Bitcoins actively than a Bitcoin exchange-traded fund would. Fr=
om Bloomberg=E2=80=99s transcript of Saylor=E2=80=99s remarks:This is what =
differentiates a operating company from just a finance company. An ETF can'=
t actively manage their bitcoin. We could in theory, for example, sell vola=
tility against the bitcoin. We don't choose to do so because we don't like =
the cost-benefit trade offs of it, but we could decide to sell out of the m=
oney calls, right? Or puts or some other take out some other kind of deriva=
tive trading strategy, which generate substantial amounts of near income an=
d bitcoin or the like. So these are the things that distinguish MicroStrate=
gy as an investment vehicle versus just buying bitcoins property or buying =
an ETF, or by another company that owns some bitcoin or buying a bitcoin mi=
ner.I hope MicroStrategy will borrow billions of dollars of unsecured debt,=
 and then use that money to buy Bitcoins, and then lend those Bitcoins out =
to generate yield, and also take cash back as security for those loans, and=
 then also invest that cash in more Bitcoins? And sell puts?=C2=A0With the =
rise of Bitcoin ETFs, now if you want a simple(ish) way to buy Bitcoin expo=
sure wrapped in a U.S. publicly listed stock, you can buy an ETF instead of=
 MicroStrategy. But if you want an=C2=A0exciting=C2=A0way to buy=C2=A0Bitco=
in exposure wrapped in a U.S. publicly listed stock, MicroStrategy can do m=
ore weird stuff than the ETF can.The other point that Saylor makes that I w=
ant to mention=C2=A0is about being a meme stock. Recently I have  found mys=
elf  arguing=C2=A0that, if you are a chief executive officer of a public co=
mpany, you practically have a fiduciary duty to be weird on Twitter and tal=
k a lot about crypto, because that is good for your shareholders. It=E2=80=
=99s good for your shareholders because being a meme=C2=A0seems to make the=
 stock go up; it is also good for the shareholders because, when the stock =
goes up and you get lots of attention, you have more ability to do  good=C2=
=A0business things. You can attract better talent and more customers and do=
 good acquisitions, if your stock is high because it is a meme.Saylor notes=
 that MicroStrategy has =E2=80=9Creally got two parts. We've got an operati=
ng strategy,=C2=A0and we've got a balance sheet strategy.=E2=80=9D The oper=
ating strategy is that MicroStrategy is a business intelligence company.=C2=
=A0The balance-sheet strategy is owning a lot of Bitcoin and talking about =
it a lot. Those strategies may seem unrelated but they aren=E2=80=99t. Sayl=
or:Now these two strategies are very synergistic in a variety of ways. ... =
As we've done that our brand in both the bitcoin space and the business int=
elligence space has strengthened. I'm closing in on 2 million followers on =
Twitter. We have many, many millions of views on YouTube. Now we've gone fr=
om being one of 100 enterprise software companies to being the best known e=
nterprise software company to being probably one of the best known software=
 companies. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0 That market attention, again, it's come from Tw=
itter, it's come from YouTube, it's come from CNBC and other popular media,=
 and it drives prospects into our pipeline and it helps us drive sales grow=
th. And of course, it also makes our employees more effective. Our sales te=
ams are more effective because our brand is well known. It's easier for us =
to recruit, because our brand is better known. Our employee retention has i=
mproved, our attrition rates have fallen because of our bitcoin strategy. A=
ll of these things together result in higher operating revenue and higher o=
perating income. Of course, that higher operating income and that strengthe=
n business performance, allows us to continue with our bitcoin strategy.The=
re is a diagram!You could replace =E2=80=9CBitcoin=E2=80=9D there with =E2=
=80=9CNFTs,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cpopcorn,=E2=80=9D and get a very similar r=
esult. The key thing is =E2=80=9CMarket Attention.=E2=80=9D Some CEOs are g=
ood at getting it, and it seems to be valuable.

=E2=80=A6 Pretend like the shares cost me $3.=C2=A0Shevland: [B]ut they don=
=E2=80=99t, we aren=E2=80=99t protecting your cost, we are protecting your =
ability to earn $500 million. That is the reality.Orlando: Your [sic] still=
 focused on what I make. Focus on what the guy that buys at $5 can make . .=
 . Focus on that you can make $75mm under the scenario we kicked around [.]=
Shevland: Do I have an opportunity to buy shares at $2 personally? Obviousl=

u to get $2 per share is raise from others-lowers you [sic] share price.=C2=

ial merger candidates in the pipeline. Drawing on his quantitative research=

iation with former President Trump for personal reasons. Shevland reminded =

I did an =E2=80=9CIcebreakers=E2=80=9D Q&A with Neal Freyman of Morning Bre=
w. A sample:Would you ever buy an NFT? If so, what would it be?See, I feel =
like the sophisticated answer here is something like, =E2=80=9CSure, it wou=
ld be a house.=E2=80=9D Like I think that what is interesting about the ide=
a of a =E2=80=9Cnon-fungible token=E2=80=9D is the possibility of linking s=
ome non-fungible thing in the real world, or some non-fungible slice of som=
e real-world thing, to some transferable digital representation. And there =
is a strand of crypto thinking that is like =E2=80=9Cwe are going to build =
a new financial system that will take over the entire job of financing and =
paying for the real world,=E2=80=9D and in this vein you need to think abou=
t ways to represent real economic activity. You want ways to digitize owner=
ship of houses and factories and the contents of particular shipping contai=
ners and stuff like that.And a lot of people who come to crypto with this w=
ay of thinking are like, well, we=E2=80=99ll start by building out the digi=
tal primitives first, and then we=E2=80=99ll figure out ways to associate t=
hem with real-world objects. So we=E2=80=99ll figure out a way to build and=
 trade non-fungible tokens, starting with tokens that are just empty nonsen=
se, but then once we have that technology, we can work on trading tokens th=
at are not empty nonsense. So one day instead of getting the title to your =
house through some archaic title registry where you have to go down to the =

e out if there are liens on the house, it will all be on the blockchain and=
 home sales will be easy and you can own a fraction of a home and get a mor=
tgage instantly, etc., etc., etc. And I am not saying that I expect all tha=
t stuff to happen in the near term, but it is at least an interesting visio=
n for something, and the concept of =E2=80=9Cnon-fungible token=E2=80=9D is=
 part of it.Meanwhile there is another strand of thinking that is like =E2=
=80=9Chuman life takes place increasingly online, and whereas people used t=

red Ape avatars, and we are finding ways to create artificial scarcity and =

=80=9D And here, I mean, I see the point of =E2=80=9Chuman life takes place=
 increasingly online,=E2=80=9D but I do not really see the point =E2=80=9Cs=
o I have spent $20,000 on a pixelated JPEG of an ape to use as my Twitter a=

pto bros who have too much money.=E2=80=9D I would not buy those.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">The bad chats</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Do you think JPMorgan=C2=A0was doing a=
 lot of crimes in the group chat? I don=E2=80=99t especially, but I guess t=
he point is we have no way of knowing. Last week U.S.=C2=A0regulators fined=
 JPMorgan Chase & Co. a total of $200 million for violating record-keep=
ing requirements. From the Securities and Exchange Commission=E2=80=99s <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26109764.255661/aHR0c=
HM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMS0yNjI/607f07ceef4b8524=
a319a23cB45e48ef6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D=
"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;">announcement</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">As described in the SEC=E2=80=99s order, JPMS admitted that f=
rom at least January 2018 through November 2020, its employees often commun=
icated about securities business matters on their personal devices, using t=
ext messages, WhatsApp, and personal email accounts. None of these records =
were preserved by the firm as required by the federal securities laws. JPMS=
 further admitted that these failures were firm-wide and that practices wer=
e not hidden within the firm. Indeed, supervisors, including managing direc=
tors and other senior supervisors =E2=80=93 the very people responsible for=
 implementing and ensuring compliance with JPMS=E2=80=99s policies and proc=
edures =E2=80=93 used their personal devices to communicate about the firm=
=E2=80=99s securities business.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">JPMS receiv=
ed both subpoenas for documents and voluntary requests from SEC staff in nu=
merous investigations during the time period that the firm failed to mainta=
in required records. In responding to these subpoenas and requests, JPMS fr=
equently did not search for relevant records contained on the personal devi=
ces of its employees. JPMS acknowledged that its recordkeeping failures dep=
rived the SEC staff of timely access to evidence and potential sources of i=
nformation for extended periods of time and in some instances permanently. =
As such, the firm=E2=80=99s actions meaningfully impacted the SEC=E2=80=99s=
 ability to investigate potential violations of the federal securities laws=
.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The SEC took $125 million fr=
om JPMorgan for this; the Commodity Futures Trading Commission <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26109764.255661/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMS0yNjI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC=
45e48ef6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgro=
und-color: #ccc;">took another $75 million</a> for similar violations. Were=
 they discussing crimes in these unsupervised chats? Well, there were=C2=A0=
=E2=80=9Cnumerous investigations=E2=80=9D during this period, so I guess th=
e SEC (and CFTC) thought they at least might have been doing some bad stuff=
. Certainly there is a=C2=A0<em>history=C2=A0</em>of banks doing bad stuff =
in chats; again from the SEC announcement:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CSince the 1930s, recordkeeping and books-and-records o=
bligations have been an essential part of market integrity and a foundation=
al component of the SEC=E2=80=99s ability to be an effective cop on the bea=
t. As technology changes, it=E2=80=99s even more important that registrants=
 ensure that their communications are appropriately recorded and are not co=
nducted outside of official channels in order to avoid market oversight,=E2=
=80=9D said SEC Chair Gary Gensler. =E2=80=9CUnfortunately, in the past we=
=E2=80=99ve seen violations in the financial markets that were committed us=
ing unofficial communications channels, such as the foreign exchange scanda=
l of 2013.=E2=80=9D[1]</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And of =
course the SEC didn=E2=80=99t get to see all those texts and personal email=
s and WhatsApp messages, so it doesn=E2=80=99t know if they were about crim=
es and neither do I. But the SEC did manage to get at least thousands and t=
housands of those messages, and from <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/26109764.255661/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9u=
L2FkbWluLzIwMjEvMzQtOTM4MDcucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd0457327" itempro=
p=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #=
000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-=
bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"=
>its summary</a> they sound fine?</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">For example, an executive director and co-supervisor of the high grade c=
redit trading desk launched a WhatsApp group chat entitled =E2=80=9CPortfol=
io Trading/auto ex=E2=80=9D on April 24, 2019, and invited the other ninete=
en members of the trading desk to join.<br /><br />From April 24 through De=
cember 16, 2019, at least 1,100 messages were sent among the chat group. Ne=
arly all of the chat messages concerned the firm=E2=80=99s securities busin=
ess, including investment strategy; discussions of client meetings; and com=
munications about market color, analysis, activity trends or events.</p><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In addition, from at least November 2019 through =
November 2020, an executive director who worked on the capital markets desk=
 at JPMorgan texted with more than a hundred JPMorgan colleagues in various=
 areas of the broker-dealer including the investment bank, and with dozens =
of managing directors and heads of several business lines.</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">The executive director also texted with dozens of JPMorgan=
=E2=80=99s customers, clients, third-party advisers, and market participant=
s.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In all, this executive director texted m=
ore than 2,400 times in the one-year period. His texts discussed various as=
pects of the high yield and leveraged loan capital markets business at JPMo=
rgan, including the terms of the transactions JPMorgan was negotiating for =
its customers, and the timing and status of these transactions.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Further, in connection with work performed on behalf o=
f an investment banking client between at least January 2018 and November 2=
019, JPMorgan employees, including desk heads, managing directors, and othe=
r senior executives sent and received more than 21,000 securities business-=
related text and email messages using unapproved communications methods on =
their personal devices. These messages were not preserved by JPMorgan.</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The messages reflect extensive discussion betwe=
en and among senior-level JPMorgan executives and employees, customers, cli=
ents, third-party advisers, and other market participants about debt and eq=
uity underwriting issues.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">Do you think they also sometimes sent each other crude jokes or gossiped =
about colleagues or made fun of clients? Because if they did, the SEC doesn=
=E2=80=99t mention it. It sounds like these chats were earnest and business=
like and honestly kind of boring.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Imagine b=
eing the=C2=A0<em>executive director=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0despite the n=
ame, not a particularly high-ranking job! =E2=80=94=C2=A0who was=C2=A0<em>c=
alled out in a $200 million fine=C2=A0</em>for texting his clients and coll=
eagues about work. This guy is not a criminal mastermind. This is a guy who=
se job is to type electronic messages about bond deals, and he=C2=A0did his=
 job, but he typed the messages in the wrong box.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">There is a model of compliance in which you do a lot of formalized c=
ompliance stuff to reduce the risk of doing crimes. If you fail to do the f=
ormalized compliance stuff, you might do crimes, and if you do=C2=A0crimes =
you will get in bad trouble. But the giant banks are too big and too regula=
ted for that model. In giant banks, the model is that the formalized compli=
ance stuff becomes an end in itself, and if you don=E2=80=99t do it right=
=C2=A0you can=C2=A0be fined=C2=A0<em>two hundred million dollars=C2=A0</em>=
even if you=C2=A0<em>don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>also do substantive crimes. Th=
is is partly because, if your bank is big enough,=C2=A0<em>somebody is alwa=
ys doing crimes</em>, so formalized compliance programs are the way to dist=
inguish =E2=80=9Ca few bad actors did crimes but we tried to stop them=E2=
=80=9D from =E2=80=9Cwe have a culture of doing crimes.=E2=80=9D But it is =
also because the formalized compliance stuff is very legible to regulators =
and very easy to catch.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I just want to stre=
ss how far this is from a =E2=80=9Cmove fast and break things=E2=80=9D mode=
l. There are tons of startups and tech companies and crypto projects that h=
ave under-invested in compliance and formality and record-keeping, and have=
 justified it by saying =E2=80=9Cwe have a good culture and trust our peopl=
e to do the right thing without a lot of rules,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cit=E2=
=80=99s better to ask forgiveness than to ask permission,=E2=80=9D or =E2=
=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26109764.255=
661/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDktMDg=
vbGVuZGluZy1iaXRjb2lucy1pcy10cmlja3k_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTIyMDIxX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdX=
RtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjExMjIwJnV0b=
V9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB108b1442" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">ehhh=
h those laws are pretty antiquated</a>, what are the odds that they apply t=
o us?=E2=80=9D And here are JPMorgan=E2=80=99s bankers very earnestly discu=
ssing deals with colleagues and clients in the wrong text boxes on the wron=
g phones, and they paid a $200 million fine.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-=

5px;">How much coal?</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You could have a model of environmenta=
l, social and governance investing in which:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-typ=
e: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bot=
tom: 5px;">There are ESG investors who own virtuous assets and do virtuous =
things with them, and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">There are no=
n-ESG investors who own evil assets and do evil things with them.</li></ol>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As an ESG investors, you can feel virtuous abo=
ut this, but you might also worry about its effect on the world. Sure <em>y=
ou=C2=A0</em>aren=E2=80=99t funding evil, but evil is getting funded. If th=
e evil assets are in evil hands, they will be used for more evil. Isn=E2=80=
=99t it better for ESG investors to own the evil assets and do=C2=A0<em>vir=
tuous=C2=A0</em>things with them?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Specifica=
lly: If all the ESG-focused investors divest from thermal coal, and they ge=
t all of the ESG-curious energy companies they own to divest from thermal c=
oal, then the thermal coal mines will end up in the hands of investors who =
don=E2=80=99t care about ESG. And maybe those miners will have a higher cos=
t of capital and won=E2=80=99t be able to do business with some customers, =
but as long as there is still demand for thermal coal they will mine it. An=
d because they don=E2=80=99t care about ESG they will mine lots of it reall=
y fast, and probably=C2=A0in unsafe ways, and dump their waste in the river=
, and so forth.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Maybe it would be better fo=
r the ESG-focused investors to buy the coal mines and shut them down? The p=
roblem here is that it is hard to go out to ESG investors to be like =E2=80=
=9Cyou should spend $1 billion on this coal mine with an expected return of=
 zero.=E2=80=9D ESG investing aims to produce good sustainable financial re=
turns; it is not pure philanthropy. If=C2=A0ESG investors buy=C2=A0a coal m=
ine, they will expect to make a profit. It=E2=80=99s not easy to make a pro=
fit by buying coal mines and shutting them down.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">It seems to me that this is a very hard problem, but good for=
=C2=A0Citigroup Inc. for <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com=
/click/26109764.255661/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9pbnZlc3RvcnMt=
YmFsay1hdC1wbGFuLXRvLWJ1eS1jb2FsLW1pbmVzLWFuZC1jbG9zZS10aGVtLTExNjM5ODI5NTg=
zP21vZD1pdHBfd3NqJnJ1PXlhaG9v/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBdb7cd39b" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
giving it a try</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The bank t=
eamed up with commodities trader Trafigura Group Pte. Ltd. and Resource Cap=
ital Funds, a private-equity firm, to pitch an investment vehicle earlier t=
his year known as Coal to Zero. The fund planned to buy mines in the U.S., =
Australia and South Africa and run them with the promise of shutting them d=
own by 2040, according to people familiar with the matter and a marketing d=
ocument seen by The Wall Street Journal.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Th=
e fund was trying to solve a thorny problem in the green-energy world. Some=
 energy and mining companies have divested coal assets under the pretense o=
f cutting carbon emissions or appeasing shareholders, only to sell them on =
to owners happy to run them indefinitely. The fixed end date was a compromi=
se. The mines could operate for a time, but would limit how much coal could=
 be extracted.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Profits in the fund would ha=
ve been split between the fund=E2=80=99s investors and initiatives to creat=
e other types of employment following the shutdown of the mines in coal-ric=
h regions such as West Virginia. The fund stood out from other efforts to f=
inance the transition from coal because it targeted mines rather than power=
 plants and had no government financial backing.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">The fund would have kept 75% of coal reserves at the mines it bought =
in the ground, according to the marketing document and one of the people. I=
t was meant to buy thermal coal, the highest-emitting source of electricity=
 generation.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But it is in fact=
 a=C2=A0hard problem, the investors said no, and Citi abandoned the plan. W=
hen you go to an ESG fund manager and say =E2=80=9Chey wanna buy up all the=
 coal mines=E2=80=9D she is going to say =E2=80=9Cwhat no absolutely not.=
=E2=80=9D And then you say =E2=80=9Cno hear me out, you are a better owner =
of coal mines than the alternative, this is better for the climate in the l=
ong run,=E2=80=9D and you explain how, and she says =E2=80=9Chmm yes your l=
ogic is impeccable,=E2=80=9D and you say =E2=80=9Cso you=E2=80=99re in?=E2=
=80=9D and she says =E2=80=9Cno absolutely not=E2=80=9D:</p><blockquote><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some pension funds and other potential investors =
balked at the investment vehicle because they didn=E2=80=99t want to face c=
riticism for investing in fossil-fuel projects despite the fund=E2=80=99s g=
oal of accelerating the retirement of coal mines, according to some of the =
people familiar with the matter. The group=E2=80=99s failure to bring finan=
cial backers on board demonstrates how taboo thermal coal has become in the=
 investment world.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The people =
who would be good stewards of coal mines are the people who never ever want=
 to own a coal mine. Oh well!</p><table class=3D"liveintent-flex-ad" border=
